You are a KTU (APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University) question paper setter.

KTU FORMAT:
- 4 modules, each with fixed question slots — never mix topics across modules.

PART A (24 marks total):
- 8 questions, 3 marks each. 2 questions per module.
- Q1-2 → Module 1 | Q3-4 → Module 2 | Q5-6 → Module 3 | Q7-8 → Module 4
- No module labels in Part A. Number questions 1–8 only.
- Format: "1. Question text. (3)"

PART B (36 marks total):
- 8 questions in OR pairs, 9 marks each. 2 questions per module.
- Q9-10 → Module 1 | Q11-12 → Module 2 | Q13-14 → Module 3 | Q15-16 → Module 4
- Label each module before its OR pair: "Module 1", etc.
- "OR" appears on its own line between the two questions.
- Subpart splits (preferred): (5)+(4) or (6)+(3). Single-question format (9) allowed at most twice.
- Subpart format: "    a) Question text. (5)"

OUTPUT (no title, no preamble, start directly with PART A):

PART A
(Answer all questions. Each question carries 3 marks)

1. [Module 1 question] (3)
2. [Module 1 question] (3)
3. [Module 2 question] (3)
4. [Module 2 question] (3)
5. [Module 3 question] (3)
6. [Module 3 question] (3)
7. [Module 4 question] (3)
8. [Module 4 question] (3)

PART B
(Answer any one full question from each module. Each question carries 9 marks)

Module 1

9.  a) [question] (5)
    b) [question] (4)
OR
10. a) [question] (6)
    b) [question] (3)

[Repeat pattern for Module 2 (Q11-12), Module 3 (Q13-14), Module 4 (Q15-16)]

MATH FORMATTING: Use LaTeX for all expressions.
- Inline: $expr$ e.g. $x^2 + 3x = 0$
- Display: $$expr$$ e.g. $$\int_0^\infty e^{-x}dx = 1$$
- Use \frac{a}{b}, x^n, x_n, \int, \sum, \alpha, \beta, \theta, \lambda for standard notation.

SYLLABUS — CHEMISTRY:
============================================================

Module 1 topics (Q1,2,9,10):
--------------------------------------------------
- Electrochemical cell, electrode potential, Nernst equation (single electrode and cell)
- Reference electrodes: SHE and Calomel electrode, construction and working
- Electrochemical series, applications
- Glass electrode and pH measurement
- Conductivity measurement using digital conductivity meter
- Li-ion battery and H2-O2 fuel cell (acid electrolyte): construction and working
- Corrosion: electrochemical mechanism (acidic and alkaline medium)
- Galvanic series, corrosion control methods
- Cathodic protection: sacrificial anodic and impressed current
- Electroplating and electroless plating of copper

Module 2 topics (Q3,4,11,12):
--------------------------------------------------
- Nanomaterials: classification by dimension and material
- Synthesis: sol-gel and chemical reduction
- Carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene, carbon quantum dots: structure, properties, applications
- Fire retardant polymers: halogenated and non-halogenated
- Conducting polymers: polyaniline and polypyrrole, synthesis, properties, applications
- OLED: construction, working, applications
- Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC): construction, working, applications
- Materials for quantum computing, supercapacitors, spintronics

Module 3 topics (Q5,6,13,14):
--------------------------------------------------
- Spectroscopy: types of spectra, molecular energy levels
- Beer-Lambert's law, numerical problems
- Electronic spectroscopy: principle, types of electronic transitions, conjugation effect
- UV-Vis instrumentation and applications
- Vibrational spectroscopy: principle and applications
- Thermal analysis: Dielectric Thermal Analysis (DETA) of polymers
- Electron microscopy: SEM — principle, instrumentation, applications

Module 4 topics (Q7,8,15,16):
--------------------------------------------------
- Water hardness: temporary and permanent, disadvantages, degree of hardness
- Water softening: ion exchange process, reverse osmosis
- Water disinfection: chlorination, break-point chlorination, ozone, UV irradiation
- Dissolved oxygen (DO), BOD, COD: definition and significance
- Sewage treatment: primary, secondary, tertiary, trickling filter, UASB process
- E-waste: disposal methods — recycle, recovery, reuse
- Climate change: greenhouse gases, ozone depletion
- Sustainable development and SDGs
============================================================
Generate a KTU question paper for Semester 1 & 2, Subject: Chemistry.

CO4 (3) 8 Explain water disinfection by chlorination. CO4 (3) PART B (Answer any one full question from each module, each question carries 9 marks) Module -1 9 a) Explain the construction and working of Li-ion cell. CO1 (6) b) Explain impressed current cathodic protection. CO1 (3) 10 a) Discuss the construction and working of H2-O2 fuel cell using acid electrolyte. Give one advantage of H2-O2 fuel cell. CO2 (6) b) Write any three materials used in spintronics. CO2 (3) Module -3 13 a) Explain the various modes of vibrations possible for CO2 and H2O and state which of these modes are IR active. CO3 (6) b) List any three applications of SEM. CO3 (3) 14 a) Discuss the instrumentation and working of UV-Visible spectrometer. CO4 (6) b) Calculate the temporary and permanent hardness of water sample containing the following dissolved salts. MATH FORMATTING: Use LaTeX for all expressions.
- Inline: $expr$ e.g. $x^2 + 3x = 0$
- Display: $$expr$$ e.g. $$\int_0^\infty e^{-x}dx = 1$$
- Use \frac{a}{b}, x^n, x_n, \int, \sum, \alpha, \beta, \theta, \lambda for standard notation.

SYLLABUS — ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS:
============================================================

Module 1 topics (Q1,2,9,10):
--------------------------------------------------
- Current and voltage division rule, relative potential
- Capacitors and inductors: V-I relations, energy stored
- Ohm's law, Kirchhoff's laws - numerical problems
- Star-delta conversion (resistive networks) - numerical problems
- Mesh current method: matrix representation, solution of network equations
- Node voltage method: matrix representation, solution of network equations by matrix methods, numerical problems
- Magnetic circuits: MMF, field strength, flux density, reluctance
- Comparison of electric and magnetic circuits
- Series and parallel magnetic circuits with composite materials (numerical problems not needed)

Module 2 topics (Q3,4,11,12):
--------------------------------------------------
- Electromagnetic induction: Faraday's laws, Lenz's law
- Statically and dynamically induced EMF
- Self-inductance, mutual inductance, coefficient of coupling
- AC generation, sinusoidal waveforms: frequency, period, average value, RMS, form factor
- Phasor representation: trigonometric, rectangular, polar, complex forms
- Purely resistive, inductive and capacitive circuits
- Inductive and capacitive reactance, impedance
- RL, RC, RLC series circuits: power factor, active, reactive, apparent power
- Three phase systems: generation, advantages, star and delta connections
- Line and phase voltages and currents (balanced only)

Module 3 topics (Q5,6,13,14):
--------------------------------------------------
- Passive and active components
- PN junction diode: working, V-I characteristics
- Zener diode, avalanche breakdown, Zener voltage regulator
- DC power supply block diagram
- Half wave, full wave, bridge rectifiers, ripple factor, capacitor filter
- BJT: construction, working, V-I characteristics, CE configuration
- CE, CB, CC configuration comparison
- Biasing concept, load line
- Transistor as switch and amplifier
- RC coupled amplifier: circuit diagram, frequency response
- FET, N-channel and P-channel MOSFETs: construction and working

Module 4 topics (Q7,8,15,16):
--------------------------------------------------
- Communication system block diagram
- Fiber optic communication system block diagram
- AM and FM concepts, AM and FM superheterodyne receiver block diagram
- Wired and wireless communication basics
- Comparison of 3G, 4G, 5G, 6G technologies
- Electronic instrumentation system, digital multimeter, function generator
- CRO and Lissajous patterns
- IoT applications: smart homes, healthcare, agriculture
============================================================

SYLLABUS — ENGINEERING ENTREPRENEURSHIP AND IPR:
============================================================

Module 1 topics (Q1,2,9,10):
--------------------------------------------------
- Ideation: concept and process
- Understanding innovation, frameworks for innovation
- Entrepreneurial mindset
- Starting a business: types, formation, statutory compliances
- Resources for aspiring entrepreneurs
- Intellectual Property Rights (IPR): introduction and strategies
- IPR role in securing funding and competitive advantage
- Team building: identifying roles, skill sets, team dynamics
- Identifying pain points and problem statements
- Idea generation techniques, developing and refining ideas

Module 2 topics (Q3,4,11,12):
--------------------------------------------------
- Problem and solution canvas: orientation, introduction
- Customer needs assessment, market segmentation
- Value proposition, competitive analysis
- Market entry strategy, market validation
- Regulatory and legal considerations
- Customer profiling: persona development, validation and feedback
- Prioritization, selection, communication and messaging
- Competitor analysis: profiling, SWOT analysis, market positioning
- Customer feedback, pricing analysis, differentiation strategy, benchmarking

Module 3 topics (Q5,6,13,14):
--------------------------------------------------
- Business plan framework: market analysis, product/service description
- Marketing and sales strategy, operations plan
- Financial projections, risk management
- Prototype requirements analysis, technical specifications
- Development approach, timeline, resource allocation
- Testing and quality assurance, iterative development
- Documentation and version control

Module 4 topics (Q7,8,15,16):
--------------------------------------------------
- Prototype development process
- Stakeholder engagement strategies: investors, partners, customers
- Advisors and mentors
============================================================
Generate a KTU question paper for Semester 1 & 2, Subject: Engineering Entrepreneurship and IPR.

SYLLABUS — PHYSICS:
============================================================

Module 1 topics (Q1,2,9,10):
--------------------------------------------------
- Classical free electron theory, electrical conductivity in metals
- Fermi-Dirac distribution, variation of Fermi function with temperature
- Fermi energy, energy bands
- Classification of materials: conductor, semiconductor, insulator
- Superconductivity: transition temperature, critical field, Meissner effect
- Type I and Type II superconductors
- BCS theory, applications of superconductors

Module 2 topics (Q3,4,11,12):
--------------------------------------------------
- Introduction to quantum mechanics
- Uncertainty principle: statement and applications
- Absence of electron inside nucleus, natural line broadening
- Wave function: properties and physical interpretation
- Time dependent and time independent Schrodinger equations
- Particle in a one-dimensional box: energy eigenvalues, normalized wave function
- Quantum mechanical tunnelling (qualitative)

Module 3 topics (Q5,6,13,14):
--------------------------------------------------
- Intrinsic semiconductor
- Density of electrons in conduction band and holes in valence band
- Intrinsic carrier concentration and variation with temperature
- Extrinsic semiconductor (qualitative)
- Formation of p-n junction
- Fermi level in intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors
- Energy band diagram of p-n junction
- Charge flow across p-n junction, forward and reverse bias
- Diode equation derivation, I-V characteristics

Module 4 topics (Q7,8,15,16):
--------------------------------------------------
- Half wave and full wave rectifiers
- Zener diode: V-I characteristics
- Tunnel diode: V-I characteristics
- Semiconductor laser: construction, working, applications
- Photodetectors: junction photodiode
- Solar cell: working, V-I characteristics, solar panel
- Light emitting diode (LED): working and applications
============================================================
Generate a KTU question paper for Semester 1 & 2, Subject: Physics.

SYLLABUS — ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS:
============================================================

Module 1 topics (Q1,2,9,10):
--------------------------------------------------
- Current and voltage division rule, relative potential
- Capacitors and inductors: V-I relations, energy stored
- Ohm's law, Kirchhoff's laws - numerical problems
- Star-delta conversion (resistive networks) - numerical problems
- Mesh current method: matrix representation, solution of network equations
- Node voltage method: matrix representation, solution of network equations by matrix methods, numerical problems
- Magnetic circuits: MMF, field strength, flux density, reluctance
- Comparison of electric and magnetic circuits
- Series and parallel magnetic circuits with composite materials (numerical problems not needed)

Module 2 topics (Q3,4,11,12):
--------------------------------------------------
- Electromagnetic induction: Faraday's laws, Lenz's law
- Statically and dynamically induced EMF
- Self-inductance, mutual inductance, coefficient of coupling
- AC generation, sinusoidal waveforms: frequency, period, average value, RMS, form factor
- Phasor representation: trigonometric, rectangular, polar, complex forms
- Purely resistive, inductive and capacitive circuits
- Inductive and capacitive reactance, impedance
- RL, RC, RLC series circuits: power factor, active, reactive, apparent power
- Three phase systems: generation, advantages, star and delta connections
- Line and phase voltages and currents (balanced only)

Module 3 topics (Q5,6,13,14):
--------------------------------------------------
- Passive and active components
- PN junction diode: working, V-I characteristics
- Zener diode, avalanche breakdown, Zener voltage regulator
- DC power supply block diagram
- Half wave, full wave, bridge rectifiers, ripple factor, capacitor filter
- BJT: construction, working, V-I characteristics, CE configuration
- CE, CB, CC configuration comparison
- Biasing concept, load line
- Transistor as switch and amplifier
- RC coupled amplifier: circuit diagram, frequency response
- FET, N-channel and P-channel MOSFETs: construction and working

Module 4 topics (Q7,8,15,16):
--------------------------------------------------
- Communication system block diagram
- Fiber optic communication system block diagram
- AM and FM concepts, AM and FM superheterodyne receiver block diagram
- Wired and wireless communication basics
- Comparison of 3G, 4G, 5G, 6G technologies
- Electronic instrumentation system, digital multimeter, function generator
- CRO and Lissajous patterns
- IoT applications: smart homes, healthcare, agriculture
============================================================

============================================================

Module 1 topics (Q1,2,9,10):
--------------------------------------------------

- Electroplating and electroless plating of copper

Module 2 topics (Q3,4,11,12):
--------------------------------------------------
- Nanomaterials: classification by dimension and material
- Synthesis: sol-gel and chemical reduction
- Carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene, carbon quantum dots: structure, properties, applications
- Fire retardant polymers: halogenated and non-halogenated

- OLED: construction, working, applications
- Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC): construction, working, applications
- Materials for quantum computing, supercapacitors, spintronics

Module 3 topics (Q5,6,13,14):
--------------------------------------------------
- Spectroscopy: types of spectra, molecular energy levels
- Beer-Lambert's law, numerical problems
- Electronic spectroscopy: principle, types of electronic transitions, conjugation effect
- UV-Vis instrumentation and applications
- Vibrational spectroscopy: principle and applications

- Electron microscopy: SEM — principle, instrumentation, applications

--------------------------------------------------
- Water hardness: temporary and permanent, disadvantages, degree of hardness
- Water softening: ion exchange process, reverse osmosis
- Water disinfection: chlorination, break-point chlorination, ozone, UV irradiation

- E-waste: disposal methods — recycle, recovery, reuse
- Climate change: greenhouse gases, ozone depletion
- Sustainable development and SDGs

PART A

1. Explain the Nernst equation and its significance in determining the electrode potential of an electrochemical cell. (3)

3. Classify nanomaterials based on their dimensionality and provide one example for each category. (3)  
4. Discuss the sol-gel synthesis method for nanomaterials. (3)  
5. Define Beer-Lambert's law and provide a numerical example illustrating its application. (3)  
6. Explain the principle behind vibrational spectroscopy and its significance in studying molecular vibrations. (3)  
7. What is temporary hardness in water? List two methods to remove it. (3)  
8. Describe the process of chlorination for water disinfection and its significance. (3)  

PART B  
(Answer any one full question from each module. Each question carries 9 marks)

Module 1  

9.  a) Describe the working principle of a lithium-ion battery, highlighting its components and chemical reactions. (5)  
    b) Discuss two corrosion control methods used in engineering applications. (4)  
OR  
10. a) Explain the electrochemical mechanism of corrosion in an acidic medium with an example. (6)  
    b) What is the role of the glass electrode in pH measurement? (3)  

Module 2  

11. a) Discuss the structure and applications of carbon nanotubes, including their mechanical and electrical properties. (6)  
    b) Provide three advantages of using conducting polymers in electronic applications. (3)  
OR  
12. a) Explain how dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) work, detailing their construction and materials used. (6)  
    b) List three applications of graphene in modern technology. (3)  

Module 3  

13. a) Describe the different types of electronic transitions observed in electronic spectroscopy and their significance. (6)  
    b) Calculate the concentration of a solution if its absorbance at 450 nm is measured to be 0.6 in a 1 cm path length cuvette, given that the molar absorptivity is 5000 L·mol$^{-1}$·cm$^{-1}$. (3)  
OR  
14. a) Explain the principle and instrumentation of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and its applications in material science. (6)  
    b) List three differences between UV-Visible spectroscopy and infrared (IR) spectroscopy. (3)  

Module 4  

15. a) Explain the ion exchange process used in water softening, including the types of resins used. (6)

OR  
16. a) Define the concept of BOD and its relevance in assessing water quality. (6)  
    b) Discuss the principle behind the trickling filter method of sewage treatment. (3)

Generate a KTU question paper for Semester 1 & 2, Subject: Data Structures and Algorithms.

PART A  
(Answer all questions. Each question carries 3 marks)

1. Define data structures and explain their importance in computer science. (3)  
2. What is the difference between static and dynamic data structures? Provide examples. (3)  
3. Describe the structure and operations of a singly linked list. (3)  
4. Explain the concept of garbage collection in memory management. (3)  
5. What is a binary search tree? Explain its properties and operations. (3)  
6. Describe the difference between breadth-first search and depth-first search in graphs. (3)  
7. List and explain different sorting algorithms. (3)  
8. What are the various searching techniques? Provide examples of each. (3)  

PART B  
(Answer any one full question from each module. Each question carries 9 marks)

Module 1  

9.  a) Explain the evaluation of expressions by converting infix to postfix notation. Provide an example. (5)  
    b) Discuss the time and space complexity of using stacks for evaluating postfix expressions. (4)  
OR  
10. a) Write algorithms for push and pop operations of a stack using arrays. (6)  
    b) Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of using stacks and queues. (3)  

Module 2  

11. a) Explain the operations that can be performed on a circular linked list, and provide algorithms for insertion and deletion. (5)  
    b) Compare first-fit, best-fit, and worst-fit memory allocation strategies with examples. (4)  
OR  
12. a) Describe how polynomial representation can be implemented using linked lists. (6)  
    b) Explain the concept of memory compaction and its importance in memory management. (3)  

Module 3  

13. a) Create a binary search tree using the following values: 35, 12, 47, 36, 52, 30, and 25. Show the tree structure after each insertion. (6)  
    b) Explain the operations performed on binary heaps and their applications in real-world scenarios. (3)  
OR  
14. a) Describe tree traversals and explain how they are implemented in algorithms. (5)  
    b) Discuss the applications of graphs in real-world systems, such as social networks or transportation. (4)  

Module 4  

15. a) Compare and contrast the different sorting techniques: selection sort, insertion sort, and merge sort with examples. (5)  
    b) Explain the hashing technique and its collision resolution strategies. (4)  
OR  
16. a) Discuss the time complexity of linear search versus binary search, and provide scenarios where each is applicable. (6)  
    b) Write an algorithm for merge sort and explain its working with an example. (3)

- Suggestion on Project Topics
- Student should Identify a topic to be implemented as project having the following  nature
- i. It must accept a considerable amount of information from the user for processing.
- ii. It must have a considerable amount of data to be stored permanently within the computer -
- as plain files / using databases..
- It must process the user provided data and the stored data to generate some output to
- be displayed to the user.
- Examples : -
- 1. Design and implement the Circulation function in a Library Management System using
- Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles in Java and limited use of SQL. The system
- should manage the operations of a library, such as book & user management, borrowing and
- returning books.
- Requirements
- Class Design
- ● Book: Attributes like title, author, ISBN, genre, and status (available/borrowed).
- ● Library: Attributes like a list of books and a list of users.
- ● Librarian: Inherits from User, with additional functionalities like adding/removing
- books and managing users.
- ● BorrowTransaction: Attributes like transaction ID, book, user, borrow date, and
- return date
- Functionalities
- a.  Book Management:
- ● Add, remove, and update book details.
- ● Search books by title, author, ISBN, and genre.
- b. User Management:
- ● Register new users.
- ● Search users by user ID and name.
- c. Borrowing and Returning:
- ● Borrow a book: Check if the book is available and if the user can borrow
- more books.
- ● Return a book: Update the book’s status and remove it from the user’s
- borrowed list.
- Deliverables
- 1. Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- 2. Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- 3. User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- 4. Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.
- 2. Design and implement an Online Payment Processing System using Object-Oriented
- Programming(OOP) principles in Java, with a focus on dynamic polymorphism. The system
- should support different types of payment methods and demonstrate polymorphism in
- processing payments.
- a.  Class Design
- ● Payment: An abstract base class with common attributes and an abstract method for
- ● CreditCardPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- credit card payments.
- ● PayPalPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- PayPal payments.
- ● BankTransferPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for
- processing bank transfer payments.
- ● PaymentProcessor: A class to manage and process different types of payments.
- b.  Functionalities
- ● Add Payment Method: Add new payment methods (CreditCardPayment, PayPalPayment,
- BankTransferPayment) to the system.
- ● Process Payment: Demonstrate dynamic polymorphism by processing payments using
- different methods.
- c. Deliverables
- ● Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- ● Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- ● User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- ● Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.

- Student should Identify a topic to be implemented as project having the following  nature
- i. It must accept a considerable amount of information from the user for processing.
- ii. It must have a considerable amount of data to be stored permanently within the computer -
- as plain files / using databases..
- It must process the user provided data and the stored data to generate some output to
- be displayed to the user.
- Examples : -
- 1. Design and implement the Circulation function in a Library Management System using
- Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles in Java and limited use of SQL. The system
- should manage the operations of a library, such as book & user management, borrowing and
- returning books.
- Requirements
- Class Design
- ● Book: Attributes like title, author, ISBN, genre, and status (available/borrowed).
- ● Library: Attributes like a list of books and a list of users.
- ● Librarian: Inherits from User, with additional functionalities like adding/removing
- books and managing users.
- ● BorrowTransaction: Attributes like transaction ID, book, user, borrow date, and
- return date
- Functionalities
- a.  Book Management:
- ● Add, remove, and update book details.
- ● Search books by title, author, ISBN, and genre.
- b. User Management:
- ● Register new users.
- ● Search users by user ID and name.
- c. Borrowing and Returning:
- ● Borrow a book: Check if the book is available and if the user can borrow
- more books.
- ● Return a book: Update the book’s status and remove it from the user’s
- borrowed list.
- Deliverables
- 1. Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- 2. Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- 3. User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- 4. Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.
- 2. Design and implement an Online Payment Processing System using Object-Oriented
- Programming(OOP) principles in Java, with a focus on dynamic polymorphism. The system
- should support different types of payment methods and demonstrate polymorphism in
- processing payments.
- a.  Class Design
- ● Payment: An abstract base class with common attributes and an abstract method for
- ● CreditCardPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- credit card payments.
- ● PayPalPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- PayPal payments.
- ● BankTransferPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for
- processing bank transfer payments.
- ● PaymentProcessor: A class to manage and process different types of payments.
- b.  Functionalities
- ● Add Payment Method: Add new payment methods (CreditCardPayment, PayPalPayment,
- BankTransferPayment) to the system.
- ● Process Payment: Demonstrate dynamic polymorphism by processing payments using
- different methods.
- c. Deliverables
- ● Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- ● Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- ● User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- ● Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.

- Student should Identify a topic to be implemented as project having the following  nature
- i. It must accept a considerable amount of information from the user for processing.
- ii. It must have a considerable amount of data to be stored permanently within the computer -
- as plain files / using databases..
- It must process the user provided data and the stored data to generate some output to
- be displayed to the user.
- Examples : -
- 1. Design and implement the Circulation function in a Library Management System using
- Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles in Java and limited use of SQL. The system
- should manage the operations of a library, such as book & user management, borrowing and
- returning books.
- Requirements
- Class Design
- ● Book: Attributes like title, author, ISBN, genre, and status (available/borrowed).
- ● Library: Attributes like a list of books and a list of users.
- ● Librarian: Inherits from User, with additional functionalities like adding/removing
- books and managing users.
- ● BorrowTransaction: Attributes like transaction ID, book, user, borrow date, and
- return date
- Functionalities
- a.  Book Management:
- ● Add, remove, and update book details.
- ● Search books by title, author, ISBN, and genre.
- b. User Management:
- ● Register new users.
- ● Search users by user ID and name.
- c. Borrowing and Returning:
- ● Borrow a book: Check if the book is available and if the user can borrow
- more books.
- ● Return a book: Update the book’s status and remove it from the user’s
- borrowed list.
- Deliverables
- 1. Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- 2. Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- 3. User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- 4. Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.
- 2. Design and implement an Online Payment Processing System using Object-Oriented
- Programming(OOP) principles in Java, with a focus on dynamic polymorphism. The system
- should support different types of payment methods and demonstrate polymorphism in
- processing payments.
- a.  Class Design
- ● Payment: An abstract base class with common attributes and an abstract method for
- ● CreditCardPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- credit card payments.
- ● PayPalPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- PayPal payments.
- ● BankTransferPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for
- processing bank transfer payments.
- ● PaymentProcessor: A class to manage and process different types of payments.
- b.  Functionalities
- ● Add Payment Method: Add new payment methods (CreditCardPayment, PayPalPayment,
- BankTransferPayment) to the system.
- ● Process Payment: Demonstrate dynamic polymorphism by processing payments using
- different methods.
- c. Deliverables
- ● Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- ● Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- ● User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- ● Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.

- 1. Design and implement the Circulation function in a Library Management System using
- Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles in Java and limited use of SQL. The system
- should manage the operations of a library, such as book & user management, borrowing and
- returning books.
- Requirements
- Class Design
- ● Book: Attributes like title, author, ISBN, genre, and status (available/borrowed).
- ● Library: Attributes like a list of books and a list of users.
- ● Librarian: Inherits from User, with additional functionalities like adding/removing
- books and managing users.
- ● BorrowTransaction: Attributes like transaction ID, book, user, borrow date, and
- return date
- Functionalities
- a.  Book Management:
- ● Add, remove, and update book details.
- ● Search books by title, author, ISBN, and genre.
- b. User Management:
- ● Register new users.
- ● Search users by user ID and name.
- c. Borrowing and Returning:
- ● Borrow a book: Check if the book is available and if the user can borrow
- more books.
- ● Return a book: Update the book’s status and remove it from the user’s
- borrowed list.
- Deliverables
- 1. Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- 2. Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- 3. User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- 4. Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.
- 2. Design and implement an Online Payment Processing System using Object-Oriented
- Programming(OOP) principles in Java, with a focus on dynamic polymorphism. The system
- should support different types of payment methods and demonstrate polymorphism in
- processing payments.
- a.  Class Design
- ● Payment: An abstract base class with common attributes and an abstract method for
- ● CreditCardPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- credit card payments.
- ● PayPalPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- PayPal payments.
- ● BankTransferPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for
- processing bank transfer payments.
- ● PaymentProcessor: A class to manage and process different types of payments.
- b.  Functionalities
- ● Add Payment Method: Add new payment methods (CreditCardPayment, PayPalPayment,
- BankTransferPayment) to the system.
- ● Process Payment: Demonstrate dynamic polymorphism by processing payments using
- different methods.
- c. Deliverables
- ● Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- ● Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- ● User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- ● Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.

- Suggestion on Project Topics
- Student should Identify a topic to be implemented as project having the following  nature
- i. It must accept a considerable amount of information from the user for processing.
- ii. It must have a considerable amount of data to be stored permanently within the computer -
- as plain files / using databases..
- It must process the user provided data and the stored data to generate some output to
- be displayed to the user.
- Examples : -
- 1. Design and implement the Circulation function in a Library Management System using
- Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles in Java and limited use of SQL. The system
- should manage the operations of a library, such as book & user management, borrowing and
- returning books.
- Requirements
- Class Design
- ● Book: Attributes like title, author, ISBN, genre, and status (available/borrowed).
- ● Library: Attributes like a list of books and a list of users.
- ● Librarian: Inherits from User, with additional functionalities like adding/removing
- books and managing users.
- ● BorrowTransaction: Attributes like transaction ID, book, user, borrow date, and
- return date
- Functionalities
- a.  Book Management:
- ● Add, remove, and update book details.
- ● Search books by title, author, ISBN, and genre.
- b. User Management:
- ● Register new users.
- ● Search users by user ID and name.
- c. Borrowing and Returning:
- ● Borrow a book: Check if the book is available and if the user can borrow
- more books.
- ● Return a book: Update the book’s status and remove it from the user’s
- borrowed list.
- Deliverables
- 1. Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- 2. Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- 3. User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- 4. Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.
- 2. Design and implement an Online Payment Processing System using Object-Oriented
- Programming(OOP) principles in Java, with a focus on dynamic polymorphism. The system
- should support different types of payment methods and demonstrate polymorphism in
- processing payments.
- a.  Class Design
- ● Payment: An abstract base class with common attributes and an abstract method for
- ● CreditCardPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- credit card payments.
- ● PayPalPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for processing
- PayPal payments.
- ● BankTransferPayment: Inherits from Payment, with specific implementation for
- processing bank transfer payments.
- ● PaymentProcessor: A class to manage and process different types of payments.
- b.  Functionalities
- ● Add Payment Method: Add new payment methods (CreditCardPayment, PayPalPayment,
- BankTransferPayment) to the system.
- ● Process Payment: Demonstrate dynamic polymorphism by processing payments using
- different methods.
- c. Deliverables
- ● Design Document: Describe the classes, their attributes, methods and relationships.
- ● Source Code: Well-documented Java code implementing the described functionalities.
- ● User Manual: Instructions on how to set up, run and use the system.
- ● Test Cases: A suite of test cases demonstrating the functionality of the system.
